items.correct: Ellipses (Kepler's first law), with the Sun at one focus.
items.why: Students think orbits are circular. Most are nearly circular, but Kepler showed they're ELLIPTICAL - this small difference explains why Earth-Sun distance varies through the year.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.
Correct answer: 13.8 Gyr. “4.5 Gyr” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 380,000 years. “1 second” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Age of the universe in billions of years (best estimate, rounded)?
items.why: The real figure is 14 Gyr. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
